title: Scientists Found A Hole In The Universe 330 Million Light Years Wide
hook: Point a telescope into a patch of the constellation Bootes and you'll find almost nothing there. Astronomers call it, only half-jokingly, the Great Nothing.
body: The region is about 330 million light years across, and a typical area that size should contain roughly two thousand galaxies. This one has fewer than sixty. It isn't empty because of a mistake in our instruments; multiple independent surveys have confirmed the gap. The leading explanation is that this is simply an extreme example of the cosmic web, the large-scale structure where galaxies cluster along filaments with vast low-density voids in between, and Bootes happens to be one of the biggest voids we've found. What's still debated is why this particular void is so much larger and emptier than the models predict for a region its size.
cta: If a civilization somehow existed inside that void, their entire night sky would be almost completely dark. Follow for more scale-of-the-universe facts.
description: Inside the Bootes Void, a region of space roughly 330 million light years across with far fewer galaxies than it should have.
